svg.title: DSA pattern recognition decision flowchart
svg.desc: A decision tree to identify which algorithm pattern to apply for a given coding problem

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How do I analyze a new DSA problem from scratch?')
sendPrompt('What patterns work on arrays and strings?')
sendPrompt('When should I use sliding window vs two pointers?')
sendPrompt('Explain the sliding window pattern with examples')
sendPrompt('Explain the two pointers pattern with examples')
sendPrompt('BFS vs DFS for tree problems — how do I choose?')
sendPrompt('Explain tree BFS with C++ examples')
sendPrompt('Explain tree DFS with C++ examples')
sendPrompt('How do I know if a graph problem needs BFS or DFS?')
sendPrompt('Dijkstra vs BFS for shortest path problems')
sendPrompt('When to use Union Find vs DFS for graph connectivity?')
sendPrompt('How do I figure out if a problem needs dynamic programming?')
g.text: Does it ask for optimal / count / ways?
min, max, number of ways, true/false

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How do I identify DP subproblems and state?')
sendPrompt('Show me DP examples: 1D, 2D, and interval DP')
sendPrompt('What is the greedy algorithm pattern and when does it work?')
sendPrompt('How do I know when to use a heap or monotonic stack?')
sendPrompt('Heap vs monotonic stack — when to use each?')
sendPrompt('What is binary search on answer and when do I use it?')

svg.text: Click any node to go deeper → use this as a checklist while solving
Step 1: Identify input type · Step 2: Identify goal (optimal / count / search)
Step 3: Map to pattern · Step 4: Define state if DP · Step 5: Code template
When stuck on Hard: solve the brute force first, then optimize with the pattern
